sentences.text: [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]
OK.
My name is Solomon Kupitz.
And I come from a family of seven children, my father and my mother.
I was born in Krasnobrod.
K-R-A-S-N-O-B-R-O-D.
And this is actually the state of Lublin, Poland.
And I was a little baby when my father left to the United States.
And my mother was left with seven children, which I was the youngest one.
On August the 18, 1928.
And I was with my family, growing up in a home, in a house.
My mother was everything.
She was the mother, the father, and doctor if children were sick.
She was doing everything that had to be done in the house.
And the rest of my brothers and sisters, they went to school.
And we had a nice home, a friendly-- my mother was very religious.
And--
We-- you don't have to belong, but we had one big synagogue where all the children, my brothers and sisters, were going to.
And then, when I grow up, I was going to the same.
It was a beautiful synagogue in the center of town.
It was a small town.
It was not a big one.
It had a couple thousand.
And it was very beautiful, it-- the town.
And we had a lot of forests.
And I-- then, when I grew up, I went to a cheder, which was a Jewish school.
And after that, I went to public school.
When I was six years old, I start to go to school.
And my father always wrote letters to my mother.
And we hoped that someday we will all go to America and see my father.
And--
My father-- in the beginning, he used to send every month, every month and a half to my mother money.
And it was-- she managed.
She was a very fine [NON-ENGLISH], that with not money, even she could manage.
Every time-- every month it came in from my father, the money, she paid for all the things for the house.
And it was really beautiful.
My sisters, my brothers, the older ones-- they had friends.
They belonged-- already, my older brother and sister-- to Zionist organizations.
And always in our house was a house of friends.
And she was loved by everybody in my town.
And then, growing older and hoping that my father can bring us to America-- but he was not an American citizen at that time.
He became an American citizen in 1938.
And as soon as he became an American citizen, he sent affidavits to the American Embassy in Warsaw.
And he wrote already at that time letters that a fire starts to burn in Europe.
And he would like us to come out as soon as, with God's help, that he can bring us over to the United States.
But because-- the American Embassy in Warsaw was not so friendly towards Jewish emigration.
And was hard that he had to put up a lot of money to show that he can bring us over.
And he had to borrow this.
And he tried everything.
In end of 1938, in the beginning of 1939, we received already letters from American Embassy in Warsaw that he needed more affidavits.
And he tried to do that.
Before the war started in 1939, he thought that we will be over.
We'll come over.
And the American Embassy, only two weeks before the war started, notified my mother that there is a visa for all of us to go to the United States.
But it happened that, within two weeks, the war broke out, and the Germans attacked.
And that was the end.
In September, just before the holidays-- of the Jewish holidays of Rosh Hashanah, the Germans marched in my town.
Yes.
There was already-- I remember when I went to school, they called us names, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and so on, many of children in school.
And also they start to boycott Jewish stores, not to buy by the Jews.
And there was already starting a lot of anti-Semitism.
I, actually, was young.
So I did not understand it so much.
But I felt it already that something is going to happen.
And when-- as I mentioned before, when the Germans came in, it was Rosh Hashanah.
And we were at the synagogue.
And the Germans came in with tanks or motorized trucks.
We had a road which came in from another town.
This name of the town was Zamosc, along towards us.
But they did not bother the Jewish people right away.
We came home.
We closed ourselves in the house.
And they were coming in the whole night.
We heard the tanks and trucks going through the city.
And a couple days later, it's quiet-- down.
The Germans bombed cities around-- not far from us.
And I saw already that it's coming, like, to an end.
So they told us to close the windows against bomb shells, and so on.
And we were going up into hiding in the house and waiting, that the Germans actually will march in completely.
Of bombing and shooting.
And the first-- yeah, by the way, when the first German tank came in, he stopped in the center of the town.
And a German officer came out, and he, he made a speech right away about the Jews.
If the Jews not going to resist and not going to do anything, if you're going to be quiet, if you're going to let us leave you alone, and we will not shot nobody.
That was the first that he gave the order.
And they left.
A week later, before Yom Kippur, actually, the Polish Army attacked the Germans in the town again, a counterattack.
And we run out of the house.
We run away because we knew that there was going to be established a front zone around there because the Polish surrounded the Germans.
So we run away in the morning, the whole family.
There were other Jewish people in our town, which came in already from west of Poland, running towards the Russians' borders, or whatever.
And they all run with us.
And as we came in into-- we were surrounded by mountains, around small mountains.
And we laid down there to be able to save our life to run away from the town, which was only a mile away.
And that-- we stayed there a whole day.
And then we heard already shooting.
And the Germans came very close.
As they came close, it was at night.
And we heard the shooting right on top on us.
We were under the mountain.
And they were on top of us already, the German regular army.
And we-- right away, at that time, we saw the city burning, the whole city afire.
It was like Rome was burning.
The whole city was in flames.
And we glad that we run away.
We stayed on that mountain for two days.
At, at, at the bottom-- on the bottom.
Actually, when they-- when the Germans already attacked and came close to us, my sister-- one of my sisters-- was in a-- a little bit away from us.
And where they-- where she was laying down, a grenade was thrown in by Germans.
And she was almost killed.
So a German saw her laying there, only to hand out from that dirt.
And he took her out.
And he brought her to us.
As we were staying there a couple days on-- in the mountains until the city was burning completely out, and--
We, we took with us whatever.
We knew that we don't know how long we have to stay.
We-- whatever we had, we took with us, and we ate-- some bread, some potatoes, whatever we could.
And then, we thought, well, how long are we going to stay?
The Germans were not near us anymore.
So we went back to the town.
As we came back, I-- we didn't recognize no more the town.
Only chimneys-- everything was burned complete to the ground.
Our house was completely burned.
But we only saw some bodies-- complete burned bodies laying not far from our house.
And there were two people, actually relatives of mine, father and a son, and also an uncle-- unrecognition.
They were all burned completely.
And my brothers-- they took them away.
They put them in a-- in a bag.
And they put them into a grave somewhere.
And we stayed in that-- there was only left over a couple houses.
So some of the houses we knew who owned them.
And we came into this-- to this friend of ours.
And we stayed there a week or two.
And the Germans were again.
And then, somebody said that the Russians are marching in from the other side of our town.
So me and my older brother-- which was about 18 kilometers away, the city was the name Tomaszow.
And we run towards the Russians as it--
To this town to, to be able to see the Russians, to run away from the Germans.
And as we came near to that town, we saw already Russians.
And we came in the house.
We had relatives over there.
And we stayed there.
And then, my rest of my family came in Tomaszow.
And they were all Russians.
And we were very happy that at least we run away from the Germans.
And maybe we will survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, we stayed for a week in that city, Tomaszow.
And the Russians said that this city might be given back to the Germans.
So we should rather go more east towards the Russian real border.
So then, my mother, my, my-- rest of my family came, and we went more east in Rawa Ruska, that the city was-- that was already in the-- part of the Ukraine.
But it was Western Ukraine, which was occupied by the Russian Army-- by the Red Army.
As we came over there, there was thousands of refugees, thousands.
We didn't have nowhere to be.
But we stayed in a school over there.
And then, it was impossible to be there.
It was sicknesses and everything.
So I remember that it was on a Friday night.
We didn't have nothing to eat, and I was going to knock a door to a Jewish family.
Maybe they can give me something.
And they locked the door.
They didn't give me even water to drink.
So after a week or two, somehow, my sister-- my-- of my younger sisters-- her name was Hannah, Hannah.
And she got a job by the Russians within a bakery.
And she used to bring home bread.
And then she said that she found out that we should get out of Rawa Ruska and go to a little town, what used to be before Germans used to live.
And we went there.
We went there.
And--
I don't remember the name.
It was around Rawa Ruska.
It was a German colony before the war.
But the Germans run away from over there.
In, in Polish Ukraine, which they used to call it-- today, they call Galicia.
But it was the Ukraine, the Western Ukraine.
And over there, we stayed a week or two.
And the Russians came in.
And they said to us that this town will be the border between the new German border and the, the Russian border, Soviet Union.
And we will have to get out.
So they offered us-- they organized a, a train with thousands of people.
And we should be the first to be evacuated and go into the regular Soviet Union, into the Ukraine.
And my mother, with my sisters and brother, they agreed with that.
And we went in-- into that train, which was not far from Rawa Ruska.
And it was, like-- it was still in 1939, after the holidays.
It was already, like, September, October, snowing and raining.
And they took the whole train, took a couple weeks, into the Ukraine, into the Soviet Union.
We were a couple of weeks on the train, maybe a week or two going because we stopped.
There was a lot of military.
Wherever there was a station, we stopped on the road.
So it took so long.
It was a, like-- it was a freight train, you know, regular freight.
And we were put in there, a lot of people, in the train.
They gave us something to eat.
Pardon me?
The Russians.
The Russians, yes.
And we came in-- into the Soviet Ukraine.
The name of the city we came in was Zhmerinka.
This is actually between Kiev and Odessa.
Zhmerinka?
Z-- I will say Z-M-- A-- E-R-- R-K-- no, R-E-K-A, Zhmerinka.
And we came in at night.
And the Russian-- they came to welcome us with music.
And they were very friendly.
And many Jewish families-- Russian Jewish families, took families to their house.
And we were going to a house in Zhmerinka.
A family-- a Jewish family-- there was a husband and wife and two daughters-- and they took us in.
They were very warm to us.
They gave us to eat.
They washed and everything.
And we were just lucky to be with them.
And then-- we stayed with them maybe a month until other people came, government people from other towns.
And they wanted every family in a different town, not everybody in the city of Zhmerinka.
So they came-- people from Brailov, which is eight kilometers away from Zhmerinka.
And they took my family, which was my mother, my sister, who was married, with her husband, then my other two sisters, and my other two brothers.
By the way, I did not mention that I had already one brother who came to the United States in 1935.
That, I didn't mention before because my brother-- this was the oldest brother.
And a girl came from New York.
And she married him.
And he went with her to the United States in 1935.
So it was only my oldest sister with her husband, then my other two sisters, Bina, Hannah, and then my brother, Avraham, Shaja, and me.
So when we came to that little town-- which they were also very friendly to us.
And they gave us an apartment.
And my older brothers and sisters-- they give them a job.
They worked in factories, tailoring.
And they went to school.
And I went to school in the Ukrainian school.
Did you speak the language?
I spoke-- not right away, but I learned fast.
They spoke Ukrainian and Russian.
And I went to this public school there in 1939 until 1941.
And my mother tried to get in contact with my father in the United States.
So the people, her friends, said, don't write to the United States if you don't want to be sent away to Siberia because we don't have any connections with America.
She did not listen.
And she wrote a letter to my father.
And my father found out right away that we are in the Soviet Union.
He tried again to make affidavits and to bring us over directly from the Soviet Union in-- between 1939 and 1940 and '41.
At that time, he also wrote if we would have had that chance to run away to Romania, he would have been able to bring us over.
But because we are in the Soviet Union, he does not know if he'll be able to do that.
But my mother tried.
And we wrote-- she wrote letters.
She was not afraid, and she wrote letters.
And he sent packages, clothing.
They had to be everything new.
Because at that time under the Soviet Union, you're not supposed to send anything old.
So he brought over here fabrics and other things and shoes.
And he sent to us.
And we were happy to know and to hear that my father is thinking about us and he knows where we are.
So we lived until 1941.
Brailov.
Brailov, B-R-A-L-O-V, Brailov.
It was a small town.
The people were friendly.
And actually, the, the government, the people of the government-- there were a lot of Jewish people.
And they were also very friendly to us.
My older sister's brother, the one who had married, my brother-in-law, he had also a good job in a factory.
He was a good tailor.
And they liked very much the family.
Then-- but we were always thinking how to get out and to go to our father.
In 1941, my father again sent affidavits to the American Embassy in Moscow.
And that-- and then, the American Embassy wrote my mother a letter that there is a visa for my mother and the youngest child.
If we-- that means if she, my mother, and the youngest, which was me, could go to the United States, but not the rest of them because I was the youngest.
And they invited my mother to Moscow.
But my mother couldn't go.
She was sick.
So my brother-in-law went to Moscow.
And he went in the name of my family to talk what can be done that the family should get a visa and get out.
When-- he came back.
And that's what they told him, that my mother and me could go if we wish.
And my mother said no.
Without the rest of the children, she will not go.
And as soon-- that was already in May.
And as soon as he came back-- I mean, we still got a letter from my father.
And in June-- I don't remember exactly the day-- June 18 or 15-- the Germans attacked the Soviet Union.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, [SIGHS] we knew already-- this is it.
As the Germans attacked, the Russians, in the beginning, tried to talk to the people, especially the Jewish people.
It was actually a speech by Stalin, or Molotov, that the Jews should be evacuated.
But many did.
We lived a little bit far from the main station of Zhmerinka.
We didn't have the train close.
And my mother was like, she didn't feel like to run no more.
But my older brother-- my older brothers and my brother-in-law was taken in-- into the army.
And they had to leave.
So I was left with my sisters and my mother.
And we saw a lot of Russian Army running away, and the Jewish people trying, with whoever could, to run.
But the Germans came in attacking all the time with planes.
And we just stayed.
And this was our destiny.
We cannot go nowhere.
We didn't have money.
And my mother wasn't feeling good.
And my brothers was not with us anymore.
And that's what it-- it was around that time.
And soon, soon the Germans start already to attacking and coming in to my town in Brailov.
In the beginning, when they came in--
That was in 1941, in, I would say, in September, before the holidays-- before the Jewish holidays in September.
And when they came in, in the beginning-- it was a small town.
They were not-- yeah, and the Ukrainians, the population-- they already showed who they are.
Like yesterday, I was with Ukrainian boys in school, and they were my friends.
We slept together.
We did not know the different.
But when the Germans came in, they didn't look anymore at me.
They were carrying guns.
And they did not kill right away, but they were waiting for the orders by the Germans.
The Germans came in a couple months later, which was already, like, in October 1941-- October, November.
By November, the SS and Gestapo came to, to our town.
And they established a headquarters.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, my brother-in-law, my brother came back from the army.
They were captured on the way, not far from Kiev.
And they returned.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
When they returned-- and what could they do?
The Germans started to take the Jews already.
They had to find out where the Jews live to work, to clean, to push trucks.
Because there was a lot of mud, a lot of trucks.
And my brothers-- I was trying to hide myself.
They didn't take me.
But they took my older brothers, my sister, my brother-in-law to do this kind of work.
They were hitting them.
And when-- once I remember that my brother-in-law came home.
He said he would rather die than to go again to do what he has to do.
And so--
Yeah.
